mnemonics: P D Q (S) - Please Dress Quietly (for Summer) - to remember the seasonal (P, D, Q) and non-seasonal (p, d, q) order parameters.
Seasonal Lags are like Holidays - They come back every year (S periods) to remind you to use the B^S operator.
MULTIPLY, Don't ADD - Remember that the seasonal and non-seasonal polynomials are multiplied together, not added, in the general ARIMA model.
keyPoints: A general multiplicative seasonal ARIMA model is denoted as ARIMA(p,d,q)×(P,D,Q)_S, combining non-seasonal (p,d,q) and seasonal (P,D,Q) components with a seasonal period S.
The backshift operator B^S is used for seasonal lags. For example, a seasonal AR(1) term would be (1 - ΦB^S)Y_t, meaning Y_t is regressed on Y_{t-S}.
The full model's AR and MA polynomials are formed by multiplying the non-seasonal and seasonal polynomials together, e.g., φ_p(B)Φ_P(B^S) for the AR part.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to difference both seasonally (D) and non-seasonally (d), leading to a non-stationary series and invalid model results.
Misidentifying the seasonal period S (e.g., using 12 for monthly data instead of 12, or 4 for quarterly data instead of 4), which completely misdefines the model's structure.
Adding the seasonal and non-seasonal components instead of multiplying them, which misrepresents the model's formulation and its interaction terms.
